hip-hop-rap.summary: One of the strongest mainstream genre families on current global streaming charts.
hip-hop-rap.whyLocal: Hip-hop works as a baseline for local genre discovery because many countries place their own local rap scenes near the top of their charts.

pop.summary: The global pop genre that anchors most country-level charts.
pop.whyLocal: Measuring the relative presence of local genres requires comparing them against a large reference genre like pop inside the same ranking.

k-pop.summary: A global genre built on the Korean idol and pop industry that regularly enters charts worldwide.
k-pop.whyLocal: K-pop is a domestic genre in South Korea but an imported one almost everywhere else, which makes it a useful lens for regional spread.

r-and-b.summary: A vocal-led mainstream genre with global reach.
r-and-b.whyLocal: R&B overlaps heavily with hip-hop and pop, so treating it as its own axis makes chart-based genre comparison more informative.

dance-electronic.summary: A global electronic music family rooted in club and festival culture.
dance-electronic.whyLocal: On country charts it often blends into pop, but it remains the reference genre for tracking regional club sounds.

rock-alternative.summary: The band-driven rock and alternative genre family.
rock-alternative.whyLocal: Japan, Europe, and the United States each feed distinct local band scenes into their charts, so rock reads differently by market.

country.summary: Country and country-pop, a genre with outsized streaming chart influence in North America.
country.whyLocal: It dominates in the United States chart while looking strongly local from every other region, which makes the contrast easy to measure.

reggaeton-latin-urbano.summary: The core urban music family of Latin American and Spanish-language charts.
reggaeton-latin-urbano.whyLocal: It is global and hyper-local at the same time: many countries chart their own variants, which makes chart-based comparison valuable.

latin-pop-musica-latina.summary: A chart-leading family covering Spanish-language pop, urbano, and tropical music.
latin-pop-musica-latina.whyLocal: Global service metadata often groups tracks as Música Latina before they split into subgenres like reggaeton or corridos.

corridos-tumbados-musica-mexicana.summary: Música Mexicana built on the corridos tradition fused with trap and hip-hop sensibilities and a young streaming audience.
corridos-tumbados-musica-mexicana.whyLocal: It holds top chart positions in Mexico and the US Latin market, with instrumentation, vocal tone, and regional identity distinct from global pop or Latin urbano.

salsa-tropical.summary: The tropical family covering salsa, cumbia, merengue, and other Latin dance and party music.
salsa-tropical.whyLocal: Long-standing regional dance genres keep re-entering Latin charts through new songs and remakes, but they hide easily inside urbano-heavy rankings.

afrobeats-afropop.summary: The modern African pop family that spread to global charts from Nigeria outward.
afrobeats-afropop.whyLocal: The genre is globalized, but country charts still show the concrete movement of each local scene.

bollywood.summary: The vast popular music family centered on India's Hindi-language film industry.
bollywood.whyLocal: It is a core genre in South Asian and diaspora charts, yet global pop taxonomies tend to flatten it into generic film music.

tamil-pop-kollywood.summary: The South Indian popular music axis combining Tamil film music and independent pop.
tamil-pop-kollywood.whyLocal: It belongs to a different language and film industry than Hindi Bollywood, but global charts often merge both into a single Indian music label.

opm.summary: OPM, the genre family covering Filipino popular music as a whole.
opm.whyLocal: Philippine charts are strong in local bands, ballads, and idol pop, but external services scatter these tracks into plain Pop or Rock.

dangdut-koplo.summary: Indonesia's massive live-event dance genre, strong across regional events, TV, YouTube, and streaming.
dangdut-koplo.whyLocal: Its drum patterns, melodies, and Javanese and Indonesian sensibility differ from Western pop chart grammar, so recognition outside Indonesia stays low.

mahraganat.summary: A raw, fast street-party music that emerged from urban Egypt.
mahraganat.whyLocal: Local social context, club and wedding culture, and Arabic hooks give it an entry barrier that global pop does not have.

arabic-pop.summary: The mainstream pop family of the Arabic-speaking world across Egypt, Lebanon, and the Gulf.
arabic-pop.whyLocal: When metadata arrives as broad Arabic labels rather than subgenres like Mahraganat, Khaliji, or Shilat, this genre acts as the necessary baseline.

arabesque.summary: A Turkish popular genre blending melodramatic vocals with folk and Middle Eastern sensibilities.
arabesque.whyLocal: It shows up strongly in Turkish charts next to modern pop and rap, but global services tend to file it under generic Turkish pop.

manele.summary: A Balkan-pop genre with mass popularity in Romania.
manele.whyLocal: It powers a huge wedding and party music market at home, but language barriers and taste debates keep it under-exposed abroad.

chalga.whyLocal: Its Balkan rhythms and flashy club-pop aesthetic are rarely recognized as a distinct genre outside the country.

disco-polo.summary: Poland's straightforward dance pop tied to weddings, festivals, and TV entertainment.
disco-polo.whyLocal: It is a textbook local mega-genre: high recognition at home, near-zero coverage in global music media.

barnmusik.summary: The children's and family music genre that appears repeatedly in Swedish charts.
barnmusik.whyLocal: Family listening and kids' content consumption feed directly into the chart independently of general pop taste, so it deserves its own axis.

levenslied-nederlandstalige-pop.summary: The local Dutch-language family of singalong pop, levenslied, and volkspop.
levenslied-nederlandstalige-pop.whyLocal: It is emotionally close to German-language Schlager, but it moves through its own language market and star system in Dutch charts.

luk-thung-mor-lam.summary: A country and regional folk-based genre family that is central to Thai popular music.
luk-thung-mor-lam.whyLocal: Its Isan-region language feel, folk roots, and working-class sensibility differ from Bangkok-centric pop and sound unfamiliar to outside listeners.

khaliji-pop.summary: The local pop and ballad-centered genre of the Gulf region.
khaliji-pop.whyLocal: Powerful within the Arab world, yet global charts flatten it into Arabic Pop, hiding its regional character.

shilat.summary: A chant and anthem-based local genre popular in Saudi Arabia and the Gulf.
shilat.whyLocal: Strong vocal chants and local event context make it hard to surface as its own genre inside generic Arabic Pop recommendations.

gqom.summary: A minimal, heavy South African club sound that grew out of Durban.
gqom.whyLocal: Rawer and more club-rooted than Amapiano, it receives weaker external classification than its real-world reach deserves.

fuji.summary: A percussion and chant-driven popular genre strong in Nigeria's Yoruba-speaking regions.
fuji.whyLocal: Overshadowed by the global success of Afrobeats, it remains a major axis of local events and long-form live performance culture.

chicha.summary: A Peruvian popular genre fusing psychedelic guitar with cumbia.
chicha.whyLocal: It exports less than reggaeton or salsa even within Latin music, so its strong local identity stays hard to discover.

vallenato.whyLocal: It touches Latin pop stardom, but the genre's long-form storytelling and regional roots are under-recognized abroad.

pimba.summary: Portugal's humorous, unabashed festival pop-folk genre.
pimba.whyLocal: Lyrical humor and local festival context carry the genre, so its popularity is hard to gauge without translation.

neomelodico.summary: An Italian local pop genre with strong Neapolitan melodrama.
neomelodico.whyLocal: Outside Italy it is easily filed under mainstream Italian pop, which hides it as a distinct regional genre.

enka.summary: A Japanese genre combining traditional vocal technique with popular song.
enka.whyLocal: It runs on a large generational, broadcast, and karaoke culture separate from J-pop, but global algorithms rarely recommend it.

trot.summary: A Korean popular genre tightly linked to broadcast TV, live events, and karaoke culture.
trot.whyLocal: Unlike the K-pop export image, trot is a separate domestic market with strong generational fandoms and a TV ecosystem.

sertanejo.summary: A country-pop family that ranks among Brazil's biggest popular genres.
sertanejo.whyLocal: It is enormous on Brazilian domestic charts, yet global Latin music coverage keeps it far less visible than reggaeton or urbano.

forro-pisadinha.summary: A dance-oriented popular genre family rooted in Brazil's Northeast.
forro-pisadinha.whyLocal: It commands a large party and festival market inside Brazil, but abroad it hides behind samba and bossa nova imagery.

brega-funk.summary: A street dance-pop genre fusing Northeastern Brazilian brega with funk.
brega-funk.whyLocal: Outside Brazil it is usually flattened into generic Brazilian funk, which erases the local scene's distinct identity.

campursari-pop-jawa.summary: Indonesian local pop mixing Javanese-language popular song, campursari, and koplo sensibilities.
campursari-pop-jawa.whyLocal: It serves a huge language market within Indonesia, but external taxonomies lump it into generic Indonesian pop.

schlager.summary: The German-language world's giant singalong and festival pop genre.
schlager.whyLocal: It has a large generational and broadcast market across German-speaking countries, but English-language recommendation systems barely surface it.

turbofolk.summary: The pop-folk and nightlife genre of Serbia and the Balkans.
turbofolk.whyLocal: The Balkans run a large star system for it, but the current collection has no chart from those countries, so curation supplements the signal.

maskandi.summary: A South African genre combining Zulu guitar tradition with modern popular music.
maskandi.whyLocal: It can chart strongly in South Africa, but the country's global music image centers on Amapiano, making maskandi hard to discover.

bongo-flava.summary: The Swahili pop, hip-hop, and dancehall blend centered on Tanzania.
bongo-flava.whyLocal: It is a mega-genre in East Africa, but Tanzania is not in the current collection, so seed-based exploration fills the gap.

rai.summary: The North African popular genre that spread worldwide from Oran, Algeria.
rai.whyLocal: Influential across North Africa and the French-speaking diaspora, yet on current global charts it usually dissolves into Arabic or French pop.

vietnamese-bolero.summary: A Vietnamese popular genre combining bolero-style ballads with karaoke culture.
vietnamese-bolero.whyLocal: Deeply familiar inside Vietnam and its diaspora, but rarely surfaced by Asian pop recommendations centered on K-pop and J-pop.
